title: How Did People Learn About the Declaration of Independence in 1776?
summary:
Imagine no internet, no TV, no phones. How would you find out that your country just declared independence? That's what happened in 1776. A new book by historian Emily Sneff, called *When the Declaration of Independence Was News*, tells the story.

The Declaration was adopted on July 4, 1776. That same day, a printer named John Dunlap made the first copies. They were big sheets called broadsides. These were sent to leaders and newspapers across the 13 colonies. People gathered in town squares to hear the Declaration read aloud. Many cheered and tore down symbols of the British king.

But not everyone was happy. Some people were worried about what would happen next. And the news took a long time to spread. It wasn't until January 1777 that a printer named Mary Katharine Goddard made copies that included the signers' names. By then, the Declaration was already old news.

Today, the original signed parchment sits behind bulletproof glass at the National Archives in Washington, D.C. But back then, it was just breaking news.
why_it_matters: This story shows how hard it was for news to travel before technology, and it reminds us how lucky we are to get information instantly on our phones or computers.

keywords.explanation: The official statement that said the American colonies were free from British rule.
A person who studies and writes about history.
Formally accepted or approved, like a new law or rule.

background_read: The Declaration of Independence was written by Thomas Jefferson (one of the Founding Fathers of the United States).
It announced that the 13 American colonies were starting their own country, separate from Great Britain.
Back then, news spread on horseback or by ship, so it could take weeks or months for people to hear about big events.
Article_Structure: WHO: The colonists, including printers John Dunlap and Mary Katharine Goddard, and historian Emily Sneff who wrote about it.
WHAT: The Declaration of Independence was adopted and then spread as news through printed broadsides and public readings.
WHERE: Throughout the 13 American colonies (the future United States).
WHY: To tell everyone that the colonies had declared independence from Britain.

perspectives.description: Many people were happy and cheered when they heard the Declaration, tearing down symbols of the British king.
Some people were scared about what would happen next, because war with Britain had already started.
Printers like John Dunlap and Mary Katharine Goddard worked hard to make copies and spread the news quickly.
